svg.title: Startup memory climbs and never comes back down
svg.desc: Resident memory of a JVM inside a container, sampled every 200 milliseconds. The solid red curve is the measurement: memory rises steeply from 4 mebibytes to 282 mebibytes within the first second as classes load, the heap commits and the code cache fills, and then it flattens into a plateau. Crucially, when the program stops doing work at 2.6 seconds the curve does not descend; it holds at 283 mebibytes for the remaining three seconds, because the JVM does not hand committed heap pages back to the operating system and the kernel only ever charges for pages that have been touched. A faint dashed curve shows the shape that was expected beforehand, a transient spike that rises above the steady state and then subsides; no such spike was observed. The practical consequence is that the peak becomes the resident cost, so a container limit sized from typical usage will be too small.
svg.text: the peak is the resident cost — size the limit from it
